title: Frontotemporal dementia - NHS
text:
, struggling to make the right sounds when saying a word, getting words in the wrong order, or using words incorrectly problems with mental abilities – getting distracted easily, struggling with planning and organisation memory problems – these only tend to occur later on, unlike more common forms of dementia, such as  Alzheimer's disease There may also be physical problems, such as slow or stiff movements, loss of bladder or bowel control (usually not until later on), muscle weakness or  difficulty swallowing .
These problems can make daily activities increasingly difficult, and the person may eventually be unable to look after themselves.
Read more about the symptoms of frontotemporal dementia .
Getting medical advice See a GP if you think you have early symptoms of dementia. If you're worried about someone else, encourage them to make an appointment with a GP and perhaps suggest you go with them.
The GP can do some simple checks to try to find the cause of your symptoms, and they can refer you to a memory clinic or another specialist for further tests if needed.
Read more about getting a dementia diagnosis .
Tests for frontotemporal dementia There's no single test for frontotemporal dementia.
The following may be needed to make a diagnosis:
an assessment of symptoms – it's normally helpful to have somebody who knows the person well to give an account of their symptoms, espec
